sanskrit:
बृहत्साम तथा साम्नां गायत्री छन्दसामहम्।
मासानां मार्गशीर्षोऽहमृतूनां कुसुमाकरः॥
transliteration:
bṛhat-sāma tathā sāmnāṃ gāyatrī chandasām aham |
māsānāṃ mārga-śīrṣo ’ham ṛtūnāṃ kusumākaraḥ ||
literal_meaning: Of the Sāma-veda songs I am the Bṛhat-sāma; of poetic metres I am the Gāyatrī; of months I am Mārga-śīrṣa (Nov-Dec); of seasons I am spring, the bringer of flowers.
contextual_meaning: Krishna names the aesthetic-emotional peaks within each domain—sound, speech, time, and nature—to show that the Self is tasted at the height of beauty and anticipation.  Psychologically, he is telling Arjuna: ‘Your peak moments—when music gives goose-bumps, when a poem stops the mind, when the harvest is safely in, when the first blossom appears—are not distractions from the war; they are direct pointers to the same Presence that steadies the bow.’  They are ‘thin places’ where ego thins and the Self shines through.
psychological_state: Heightened openness—cortical quieting and limbic glow—where the mind recognises its own source in beauty and seasonal hope.
trigger_scenario: You are driving at dusk, the radio plays the song that scored your first heart-break, harvest moon rises, and for ten timeless seconds you feel held—then traffic lights change and the spell breaks.
anchor_text: Notice the peak moment; there the Self is quietly present.
